LESSON PLAN
Subject: Creative Arts
Topic: West African Textile Patterns
Class: JSS2 | Duration: 80 minutes (double period) | Board: NERDC

Objective: By the end of this lesson, students will be able to:
1. Create an original Adire or Kente-inspired textile pattern using paper and dye
2. Design a repeating geometric motif that reflects a chosen cultural theme (e.g. harvest, community, celebration)
3. Produce a short written description (3 sentences) explaining the cultural meaning of their design
4. Develop a group mini-exhibition of all class designs

Materials: White cotton fabric squares (30×30 cm), fabric dye (blue, yellow, red), wax crayons, rubber stamps, rulers, brushes, washing buckets, Chioma's completed Adire sample (teacher reference).

Introduction (10 min):
Teacher Biodun shows students photographs of Yoruba Adire cloth, Ghanaian Kente weaving, and Aso-oke fabric from Osun State. Students discuss: 'What patterns do you see? What do you think they mean?' Kemi volunteers that her grandmother weaves Aso-oke for weddings.

Main Activity (55 min):
1. Design phase (20 min): Each student sketches their motif on paper first. Tunde designs an interlocking diamond pattern; Abena designs a bird of peace motif.
2. Production phase (30 min): Students transfer their design to fabric using wax resist or stamps, then apply dye. Obiora helps distribute dye buckets safely.
3. Reflection phase (5 min): Each student writes three sentences: what they created, why they chose the pattern, and what West African tradition it connects to.

Assessment (10 min):
Peer gallery walk: Students attach name tags to their finished textile squares. Class votes on the design that best represents a West African cultural value. Teacher Biodun provides individual written feedback on each piece.

Closure:
Students compose a class display title together. Homework: Bring one photo of a textile pattern from their home, market, or community to share next lesson.
reference: A NERDC Creative Arts lesson plan for JSS2 where students design and produce original West African textile patterns with cultural reflection.
